en.extract:
The Fiji goshawk (Tachyspiza rufitorques) is a species of bird of prey in the family Accipitridae. It was once considered to be the same species (conspecific) as the brown goshawk of Australia and New Caledonia. It is endemic to Fiji, where it occurs on the larger islands of Viti Levu, Vanua Levu, Taveuni, Kadavu, Gau and Ovalau. It occupies a range of wooded habitats in Fiji, from natural rainforest to coconut plantations and urban gardens and parks.


Description
The Fiji goshawk ranges in size from 30–40 cm, making it medium-sized for its genus.  It exhibits sexual dimorphism, with the females being larger than the males.  It has long legs and a long tail. The plumage is unmistakable within its range, having a grey head, back, tail and wings and dull pink undersides and collar. The plumage of the juvenile is distinct from the adult, being all over brown with a highly streaked breast.


Diet
The Fiji goshawk feeds on birds as large as pigeons, insects, introduced rodents and reptiles. and have also been recorded feeding on freshwater prawns from the family Palaemonidae, as well as freshwater fish. This species hunts both from perches or flying. Prey may be snatched with either a slow and stealthy glide or a quick flapping attack. Prey may be chased into cover and they will actively harass and attempt to flush concealed prey.


Breeding
Fiji goshawks are seasonal breeders, with the breeding season occurring between July and December. Most eggs are laid between September and October. The nest is a platform of sticks located high in a lightly vegetated tree. The typical clutch size is two to three eggs (occasionally four), of which up to two chicks are commonly fledged.


History
Although the species is today considered to be endemic to Fiji, fossil bones attributed to this species have been found on the island of 'Eua in Tonga. The species may once have occurred in the Lau group of Fiji as well. Fiji goshawks will occasionally take chickens and are often shot. They are nevertheless common and live at high densities.


References

The '''Fiji goshawk''' ('''''Tachyspiza rufitorques''''') is a species of [[bird of prey]] in the family [[Accipitridae]]. It was once considered to be the same species (conspecific) as the [[brown goshawk]] of [[Australia]] and [[New Caledonia]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Fiji]], where it occurs on the larger islands of [[Viti Levu]], [[Vanua Levu]], [[Taveuni]], [[Kadavu Island|Kadavu]], [[Gau Island|Gau]] and [[Ovalau (Fiji)|Ovalau]].<ref name ="Pratt">{{cite book|last=Pratt|first=H Douglas |author2=Bruner, Phillip |author3=Berrett, Delwyn|title=The Birds of Hawaii and the Tropical Pacific|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton|year=1987|page=356|isbn=0-691-08402-5}}</ref> It occupies a range of wooded [[habitat]]s in Fiji, from natural rainforest to coconut plantations and urban gardens and parks.<ref name ="HBW"/>

==Description==
The Fiji goshawk ranges in size from 30–40 cm, making it medium-sized for its genus.  It exhibits [[sexual dimorphism]], with the females being larger than the males.<ref name ="HBW">Thiollay, J.M. (1994): Family Accipitridae (Hawks and Eagles) ''in'' del Hoyo, J.; Elliot, A. & Sargatal, J. (editors). (1994). ''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]. Volume 2'': New World Vultures to Guineafowl. Lynx Edicions. {{ISBN|84-87334-15-6}}</ref>  It has long legs and a long tail. The [[plumage]] is unmistakable within its range, having a grey head, back, tail and wings and dull pink undersides and collar. The plumage of the juvenile is distinct from the adult, being all over brown with a highly streaked breast.<ref name ="Pratt"/>

==Diet==
The Fiji goshawk feeds on birds as large as [[pigeon]]s, insects, introduced [[rodent]]s and reptiles.<ref name ="HBW"/> and have also been recorded feeding on freshwater prawns from the family [[Palaemonidae]],<ref>{{cite journal|last=Clunie|first=Fergus|year=1980|title=Harriers fishing|journal=Notornis|volume=27|issue=2|page=114|url=http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_27-1980/Notornis_27_2.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081017232919/http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_27-1980/Notornis_27_2.pdf|archive-date=2008-10-17}}</ref> as well as freshwater fish. This species hunts both from perches or flying. Prey may be snatched with either a slow and stealthy glide or a quick flapping attack. Prey may be chased into cover and they will actively harass and attempt to flush concealed prey.

==Breeding==
Fiji goshawks are seasonal breeders, with the breeding season occurring between July and December.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Clunie|first=Fergus|year=1981|title=Nesting season of the Fiji Goshawk|journal=Notornis|volume=28|issue=2|url=http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_28-1981/Notornis_28_2.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081018060158/http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_28-1981/Notornis_28_2.pdf|archive-date=2008-10-18}}</ref> Most eggs are laid between September and October. The [[bird nest|nest]] is a platform of sticks located high in a lightly vegetated tree. The typical [[avian clutch size|clutch size]] is two to three eggs (occasionally four), of which up to two chicks are commonly [[fledge]]d.<ref name ="HBW"/>

==History==
Although the species is today considered to be endemic to Fiji, [[fossil]] bones attributed to this species have been found on the island of [['Eua]] in [[Tonga]].<ref>{{cite journal|last=Steadman|first=David|year=1993|title=Biogeography of Tongan birds before and after human impact|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|volume=90|issue=3|pages=818–822|doi=10.1073/pnas.90.3.818|pmc=45761|pmid=11607357|bibcode=1993PNAS...90..818S|doi-access=free}}</ref> The species may once have occurred in the [[Lau Islands|Lau group]] of Fiji as well.<ref>{{cite book|last=Steadman|first=David|title=Extinction and Biogeography in Tropical Pacific Bird|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2006|pages=360–361|isbn=978-0-226-77142-7}}</ref> Fiji goshawks will occasionally take [[chicken]]s and are often shot. They are nevertheless common and live at high densities.<ref name ="HBW"/>

zh.extract:
棕颈鹰（Accipiter rufitorques），又名斐济鹰，鹰科中的一种猛禽，曾一度被认为与澳大利亚和新喀里多尼亚的褐鹰同种。 但棕颈鹰是斐济的特有种，生活于维提岛、瓦努阿岛、塔韦乌尼岛、坎达武岛、恩高岛和奥瓦劳岛上。其栖息范围很大，从雨林，椰子种植园到城市公园的树上都可见。
棕颈鹰身长30-40厘米，在鹰属中算是中等身型。其是两性异形的鸟类，雌性的身型比雄性要大。其脚和尾部都很长，全身被羽毛覆盖，头、背、尾和翼都呈灰色，而胸腹和颈部侧为暗粉红色。幼子的毛色与成年不同，通体为棕色，胸脯有许多条纹。
棕颈鹰以鸟类为食，如鸽子，还有昆虫、啮齿类动物和爬行类动物。有纪录指棕颈鹰也会捕食长臂虾科的淡水大虾，和淡水鱼。总言之，棕颈鹰既会捕食鱼类也会捕食鸟类。其会通过慢速，不动声色的滑翔来捕捉猎物，或会用双翼快速拍打攻击。猎物也会跑入荫蔽的地方躲起来，然而棕颈鹰会不断地进攻以试图赶出躲藏着的猎物。
棕颈鹰是一种季节性繁殖动物，繁殖期为每年的7月至12月。雌性一般于9-12月下蛋。鸟巢主要由树脂构成，位于活树上。一般情况下，一窝蛋为2-3只（偶见4只），然而只有两只幼雏能长大并飞翔。
虽然棕颈鹰现在被认为是斐济的特有种，但在汤加的埃瓦岛曾发现与其同物种的化石。棕颈鹰也曾在斐济的Lau岛出现过。
